turn-3-prompt.text: How would you say 'I want to know'?

turn-5-prompt.text: How would you say 'I don't want to know'?

turn-7-prompt.text: How would you say 'he wants to know' or 'she wants to know'?

turn-11-prompt.text: Why doesn't he want to know?

turn-13-prompt.text: I want to know why he doesn't want to know:

turn-15-reveal.text: Very good. The word for 'yes' in Spanish is 'sí'. This is also the word for 'if'.

turn-17-prompt.text: 'I want to know if you want to know' (speaking formally):

turn-19-prompt.text: How would you say 'I want to know if he wants to invite me'?

turn-27-prompt.text: 'I want to know if she wants to come to the house to visit me':

turn-29-reveal.text: Very good. Let's practice a listening exercise.

turn-29-prompt.text: What does 'Voy a invitarlo, pero no voy a invitarlo ahora' mean?

turn-31-prompt.text: How do you say 'justification' in Spanish?

turn-35-prompt.text: 'I'm not trying to justify myself, but I want to explain something to you' (informally):

outro-1-full.text: You've learned how to use 'saber' and related phrases to express knowledge and curiosity in Spanish.

outro-2-full.text: Try forming your own sentences using 'saber' and other verbs you've learned. Practice makes perfect!

outro-3-full.text: Remember, breaking down sentences into smaller parts can help you construct more complex ideas in Spanish.
